results.title: Divided trail: a Native American odyssey
results.brief_description: Uses archival footage and stills to document the beginnings of the American Indian protest movement in Chicago during the 1970s. Three leaders of the movement recount the events which motivated them to bring public attention to the plight of the American Indian, who has left the reservation with promises of employment and a better life only to find unemployment, slums and alcoholism.

results.title: Distant islands
results.creator_credit: Bettina Maylone, Jack Long and National Film Board of Canada
results.brief_description: As in her previous film, the award-winning The Hometown, the filmmaker has used applique and embroidered tapestries to animate a story from her childhood. Distant Islands recalls a young girl’s happy summers spent sailing with her family off the coast of British Columbia. Each tapestry, meticulously stitched by hand with brightly colored yarns, evokes a memory of leisurely days at sea, drifting among the islands.

results.title: Perception structure or flow (TEFC)
results.creator_credit: Bob Nichol and National Film Board of Canada
results.brief_description: Seeing is believing, and believing is seeing, says this film, in an engaging, involving exanimation of what perception is - what causes one person to see things one way, another person in a totally different way. A film to stimulate discussion, especially in the classroom at senior levels.

results.title: Peoples of the Skeena
results.creator_credit: James Beveridge and National Film Board of Canada
results.brief_description: The Getikshan and Tsimshian Indians of northern British Columbia live in two worlds. Against the mountains and forests tower the ancient totem poles; the old graveyards reveal past history; on the reserves the traditional crafts are still practised. But in the school games, in the wedding complete with white veil and white rice, and in the sawmill we see how other ways are being adopted. As climax, the film shows the action-packed rodeo at Kispiox.

results.title: People on Sunday
results.creator_credit: Edgar G. Ulmer, Robert Siodmak and Filmstudio
results.brief_description: This pioneering low-budget film made at the very end of the silent era, was an avant-garde blend of feature and documentary. Its lyrical recording of the streetlife of everyday Berlin owes something to the cycle of ‘city symphony’ films popular in the mid-Twenties. Onto this documentary background is grafted a simple narrative focusing on the interactions between five young Berliners—all played by non-professional actors—who spend a typical Sunday together in and around Berlin. The film launched the careers of all six principal filmmakers involved, and had a huge influence on subsequent film movements including the French New Wave, Italian Neorealist and British Free Cinema movements. This restored version contains important scenes previously missing and features a new score by Elena Kats-Chernin. Written by Billie Wilder, Curt Sidomak and Robert Sidomak. Photography, Eugen Schufftan and Fred Zinnemann. Based on an original story by Curt Siodmak.

results.title: Distant voices, still lives
results.creator_credit: Terence Davies, Jennifer Howarth, British Film Institute, Channel Four and Zweites Deutsches Fernsehen
results.brief_description: This is the first feature film of Liverpudlian Terence Davies, obviously autobiographical, dealing with a family named Davies and their lives during the 1940s and 1950s. The film is divided into two parts: “Distant voices” (45 mins) centers on the wedding of Eileen, eldest of the three Davies children, and the funeral of her father, events which spark memories of the past, including the frightening war years when the city was bombed frequently. “Still lives” (39 mins), actually filmed two years after the first part, with the same actors but with a substantially different crew. It’s a seamless continuation of the recollections of their domineering and irrational father, played against music and radio programmes of the period to create an evocative and moving picture of family life in the 1940s.

results.title: People of the rock
results.creator_credit: Clarke Da Prato, Nicholas Balla, Tim Wilson and National Film Board of Canada
results.brief_description: This film follows an Inuit family’s journey to the North Rankin nickel mine. How the hunters became bulldozer and machine operators is explained. Music by Robert Fleming.

results.title: Peppimenarti
results.creator_credit: Ron Iddon, Film Australia and Leopardwood
results.brief_description: Peppimenarti is a cattle station in the Northern Territory near the Daly River, owned by First Nations people. Beginning by rounding up descendants of cattle who had strayed from other stations, the station has turned to a successful business enterprise with $1m turnover p.a. First Nations law takes precedence over European law, and all able-bodied men on the station must work, with none allowed to go on the dole. Peppimenarti stands as a shining example of the Outstation Movement, and has successfully combined western business sense with local customs.

results.title: People and power
results.creator_credit: Bonnie Sherr Klein, Peter Pearson, Barrie Howells, John Kemeny and National Film Board of Canada
results.brief_description: This film examines Saul Alinsky’s method of organising communities into effective action units based on participatory democracy. Conflict and controversy are an integral part of Alinsky’s approach. Alinsky talks about his philosophy and the dynamics of organisation.

results.title: 1945: Brave new world. 1948: Boomtime. 1947: Freedom now
results.creator_credit: Angus MacQueen, Jennifer Clayton, Sally Doganis, BBC and WGBH (Boston)
results.brief_description:
Episode number 4 of Series “People’s century: 1900-1999”.
This tape contains episodes eleven to thirteen of the ‘People’s century 1900-1999’ series. “1945: brave new world”; post war euphoria gives way to a new war - the Cold War, as the Communist East and the Capitalist West are separated by the Iron Curtain. The Occupied powers begin deciding how Europe will look following the war and Churchill visits America, warning them of the terror rising in the Soviet sphere. Berlin represents a pocket of capitalism in the communist landscape. When Russia gains itself an atomic bomb, Stalin has all the power he needs. Narrated by Veronika Hyks. “1948: boomtime”; the Marshall plan moves into full swing as America make a concerted financial effort to rebuild Europe after the war. Heavy industry is re invigorated and Europeans visit America to learn valuable lessons in productivity. As time passes confidence builds and Europe begins exporting back to America on their home turf. The consumer society would rides on a wave of mass prosperity until 1973 when the cheap oil required to facilitate progress is no longer available. Narrated by Sean Barrett. “1947: freedom now”; when Gandhi showed the world how the masses could successfully defy their colonial masters, thousands across Africa and Asia joined the fight for control of their own destinies. Africa is paid particular attention to in this episode - as the European colonial powers retreat, a lack of resources and the language barriers left by imposed European boundaries detract from the peace process. Freedom would come at the cost of civil war. Narrated by Veronika Hyks.
results.creator_credit_for_label: Angus MacQueen, Jennifer Clayton, Sally Doganis, BBC and WGBH (Boston)
